name: isolate
documentation:
# Isolate

Run any Python function, with any dependencies, in any machine you want. Isolate offers a
pluggable end-to-end solution for building, managing, and using isolated environments (virtualenv,
conda, remote, and more).


## Motivation

![XKCD 1987](https://imgs.xkcd.com/comics/python_environment.png)

The fact that nearly every piece of software uses some other libraries or some
other programs is undeniable. Each of these come with their set of dependencies,
and this chain moves forward. Once there are enough 'nodes' in the chain, then
the ["dependency mess"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dependency_hell) starts
to surface and our lives become much harder.

Python tried to solve it by recommending the "virtual environment" concept. In
theory it was designed to isolate environments of different projects, so my project
A can depend on `pandas==1.0.0` while B depends on `pandas==2.0.0` and whichever
project I choose to work with, I just activate its own environment.

Overall this was a very nice solution that did work, and still continues to work
for this use case. But as with every other scoped fix, in time other problems started
to appear that demand a much narrower scope (like defining module-level dependencies,
or even function-level ones for cloud runtimes that allow seamless integration with the
rest of your code running in a different machine).

However, unlike "virtual environment" concept, each of the projects that tried to tackle
this problem lacked a universal interface which one can simply define a set of requirements
(this might be dependencies, size of the machine that is needed to run it, or something completely
different) and can change it without any loss. Isolate is working towards a future where this
transititon is as seamless as the transition from your local environment to the remote
environment.

## Contributing

### Installing in editable mode with dev dependencies

```
pip install -e '.[dev]'
```

### Running tests

```
pytest
```

### Pre-commit

```
pre-commit install
```

### Commit format

Please follow [conventional commits specification](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) for descriptions/messages.

modules.name: isolate.connections.ipc.agent
documentation: This file defines an "isolate" agent for inter-process communication over
sockets. It is spawned by the controller process with a single argument (a
base64 encoded server address) and expected to go through the following procedures:
1. Decode the given address
2. Create a connection to the transmission bridge using the address
3. Receive a callable object from the bridge
4. Execute the callable object
5. Send the result back to the bridge
6. Exit
Up until to point 4, the agent process has no way of transmitting information
to the controller so it should use the stderr/stdout channels appropriately. After
the executable is received, the controller process will switch to the listening mode
and wait for agent to return something. The expected object is a tuple of two objects
one being the actual result of the given callable, and the other one is a boolean flag
indicating whether the callable has raised an exception or not.

classes.name: isolate.connections.ipc.agent.ConnectionWrapper
documentation: Somhow mypy can't figure out that `ConnectionWrapper`
really exists.

functions.name: isolate.connections.common._step
documentation:
A context manager to capture every expression
underneath it and if any of them fails for any reason
then it will raise a SerializationError with the
given message.

functions.name: isolate.connections.common.as_serialization_method
documentation:
Ensures that the given backend has loads/dumps methods, and returns
it as is (also convinces type checkers that the given object satisfies
the serialization protocol).

functions.name: isolate.connections.common.load_serialized_object
documentation:
Load the given serialized object using the given serialization method. If
anything fails, then a SerializationError will be raised. If the was_it_raised
flag is set to true, then the given object will be raised as an exception (instead
of being returned).

functions.name: isolate.connections.common.serialize_object
documentation:
Serialize the given object using the given serialization method. If
anything fails, then a SerializationError will be raised.

functions.name: isolate.connections.common.is_agent
documentation: Returns true if the current process is an isolate agent.

classes.name: isolate.connections.common.SerializationError
documentation: An error that happened during the serialization process.

classes.name: isolate.connections.ipc._base.ConnectionWrapper
documentation: Somhow mypy can't figure out that `ConnectionWrapper`
really exists.

classes.name: isolate.connections.ipc._base.AgentListener
documentation:
A custom listener that can use any available serialization method
to communicate with the child process.

classes.name: isolate.connections.ipc._base.IsolatedProcessConnection

methods.name: isolate.connections.ipc._base.IsolatedProcessConnection.start_process
documentation: Start the agent process.

methods.name: isolate.connections.ipc._base.IsolatedProcessConnection.run
documentation:
Spawn an agent process using the given environment, run the given
`executable` in that process, and return the result object back.

methods.name: isolate.connections.ipc._base.IsolatedProcessConnection.poll_until_result
documentation:
Take the given process, and poll until either it exits or returns
a result object.

A generic IPC implementation for running the isolate backend
in a separated process.

Each implementation needs to define a start_process method to
spawn the agent.

functions.name: isolate.connections.grpc.configuration.get_default_options
documentation:
Return the default list of GRPC call options (both for
server and client) which are set via environment variables.

Each environment variable starting with `ISOLATE_GRPC_CALL_`
will be converted to a GRPC option. The name of the option
will be the name of the environment variable, with the
`ISOLATE_GRPC_CALL_` prefix removed and converted to lowercase.

modules.name: isolate.connections._local.agent_startup
documentation: Agent process execution wrapper for handling extended PYTHONPATH.

functions.name: isolate.connections._local.agent_startup.load_pth_files
documentation:
Each site dir in Python can contain some .pth files, which are
basically instructions that tell Python to load other stuff. This is
generally used for editable installations, and just setting PYTHONPATH
won't make them expand so we need manually process them. Luckily, site
module can simply take the list of new paths and recognize them.

https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/modules.html#the-module-search-path

functions.name: isolate.connections._local._base.binary_path_for
documentation:
Return the binary search path for the given 'search_paths'.
It will be a combination of the 'bin/' folders in them and
the existing PATH environment variable.

functions.name: isolate.connections._local._base.python_path_for
documentation:
Return the PYTHONPATH for the library paths residing
in the given 'search_paths'. The order of the paths is
preserved.

classes.name: isolate.connections._local._base.PythonExecutionBase

methods.name: isolate.connections._local._base.PythonExecutionBase.start_process
documentation:
Start the agent process with the Python binary available inside the
bound environment.

methods.name: isolate.connections._local._base.PythonExecutionBase.get_env_vars
documentation:
Return the environment variables to run the agent process with. By default
PYTHONUNBUFFERED is set to 1 to ensure the prints to stdout/stderr are reflect
immediately (so that we can seamlessly transfer logs).

methods.name: isolate.connections._local._base.PythonExecutionBase.get_python_cmd
documentation: Return the command to run the agent process with.

methods.name: isolate.connections._local._base.PythonExecutionBase.handle_agent_log
documentation:
Handle a log line emitted by the agent process. The level will be either
STDOUT or STDERR.

A generic Python execution implementation that can trigger a new process
and start watching stdout/stderr for the logs. The environment_path must be
the base directory of a new Python environment (structure that complies with
sysconfig). Python binary inside that environment will be used to run the
agent process.

If set, extra_inheritance_paths allows extending the custom package search
system with additional environments. As an example, the current environment_path
might point to an environment with numpy and the extra_inheritance_paths might
point to an environment with pandas. In this case, the agent process will have
access to both numpy and pandas. The order is important here, as the first
path in the list will be the first one to be looked up (so if there is multiple
versions of the same package in different environments, the one in the first
path will take precedence). Dependency resolution and compatibility must be
handled by the user.

A customizable gRPC-based execution backend.

modules.name: isolate.connections.grpc.agent
documentation:
This file contains the implementation of the gRPC agent. The agent is a
separate process that is responsible for running the user code in a
sandboxed environment.

This file is referenced by the latest version of the `isolate` package
but then runs it in the context of the frozen agent built environment.
agent-requires: isolate[server]

functions.name: isolate.connections.grpc.agent.create_server
documentation:
Create a new (temporary) gRPC server listening on the given
address.

functions.name: isolate.connections.grpc.agent.run_agent
documentation: Run the agent servicer on the given address.

classes.name: isolate.backends._base.EnvironmentCreationError
documentation: Raised when the environment cannot be created.

classes.name: isolate.backends._base.BaseEnvironment

methods.name: isolate.backends._base.BaseEnvironment.from_config
documentation: Create a new environment from the given configuration.

methods.name: isolate.backends._base.BaseEnvironment.key
documentation:
A unique identifier for this environment (combination of requirements,
python version and other relevant information) that can be used for caching
and identification purposes.

methods.name: isolate.backends._base.BaseEnvironment.create
documentation:
Setup the given environment, and return all the information needed
for establishing a connection to it. If `force` flag is set, then even
if the environment is cached; it will be tried to be re-built.

methods.name: isolate.backends._base.BaseEnvironment.destroy
documentation:
Dismantle this environment. Might raise an exception if the environment
does not exist.

methods.name: isolate.backends._base.BaseEnvironment.exists
documentation: Return True if the environment already exists.

methods.name: isolate.backends._base.BaseEnvironment.open_connection
documentation:
Return a new connection to the environment with using the
`connection_key`.

methods.name: isolate.backends._base.BaseEnvironment.connect
documentation:
Create the given environment (if it already doesn't exist) and establish a
connection to it.

methods.name: isolate.backends._base.BaseEnvironment.apply_settings
documentation: Apply the new settings to this environment.

methods.name: isolate.backends._base.BaseEnvironment.log
documentation: Log a message.

Represents a managed environment definition for an isolatation backend
that can be used to run Python code with different set of dependencies.

methods.name: isolate.backends._base.EnvironmentConnection.run
documentation:
Run the given executable inside the environment, and return the result.
If the executable raises an exception, then it will be raised directly.

methods.name: isolate.backends._base.EnvironmentConnection.log
documentation: Log a message through the bound environment.

functions.name: isolate.backends.common.lock_build_path
documentation:
Try to acquire a lock for all operations on the given 'path'. This guarantees
that the path will not be modified by any other process while the lock is held.

functions.name: isolate.backends.common._keep_lock_alive
documentation:
Keep the lock file alive by updating its mtime as long
as we are doing something in the cache.

functions.name: isolate.backends.common.get_executable_path
documentation:
Return the path for the executable named 'executable_name' under
the '/bin' directory of 'search_path'.

functions.name: isolate.backends.common._io_observer
documentation:
Starts a new thread that reads from the specified file descriptors
and calls the bound hook function for each line until the EOF is reached
or the termination event is set.

Caller is responsible for joining the thread.

functions.name: isolate.backends.common._unblocked_pipe
documentation:
Create a pair of unblocked pipes. This is actually
the same as os.pipe2(os.O_NONBLOCK), but that is not
available in MacOS so we have to do it manually.

functions.name: isolate.backends.common.logged_io
documentation:
Open two new streams (for stdout and stderr, respectively) and start relaying all
the output from them to the given hooks.

functions.name: isolate.backends.common.sha256_digest_of
documentation:
Return the SHA256 digest that corresponds to the combined version
of 'unique_fields. The order is preserved.

functions.name: isolate.backends.common.active_python
documentation:
Return the active Python version that can be used for caching
and re-creating this environment. Currently only covers major and
minor versions (like 3.9); patch versions are ignored (like 3.9.4).

functions.name: isolate.backends.common.optional_import
documentation:
Try to import the given module, and fail if it is not available
with an informative error message that includes the installations
instructions.

methods.name: isolate.backends.settings.IsolateSettings._infer_log_level
documentation: Infer the log level if it's correctly set.

methods.name: isolate.backends.settings.IsolateSettings._get_temp_base
documentation:
Return the base path for creating temporary files/directories.

If the isolate cache directory is in a different device than the
system temp base (e.g. /tmp), then it will return a new directory
under the cache directory.

methods.name: isolate.backends.settings.IsolateSettings._get_lock_dir
documentation: Return a directory which can be used for storing file-based locks.

methods.name: isolate.backends.settings.IsolateSettings.cache_lock_for
documentation:
Create a lock for accessing (and operating on) the given path. This
means whenever the context manager is entered, the path can be freely
modified and accessed without any other process interfering.

methods.name: isolate.backends.settings.IsolateSettings.cache_dir_for
documentation:
Return a directory which can be used for caching the given
environment's artifacts.

methods.name: isolate.server.server.IsolateServicer.watch_queue_until_completed
documentation:
Watch the given queue until the is_completed function returns True.
Note that even if the function is completed, this function might not
finish until the queue is empty.

classes.name: isolate.server.server.SingleTaskInterceptor
documentation: Sets server to terminate after the first Submit/Run task.

methods.name: isolate.backends.virtualenv.VirtualPythonEnvironment.install_requirements
documentation:
Install the requirements of this environment using 'pip' to the
given virtualenv path.

If there are any constraint files specified, they will be also passed to
the package resolver.

classes.name: isolate.logs.Log
